title: Constable Mï؟½Cartyï؟½s Investigations
content:
آ آ آ آ Most unpleasantly adjacent to the haunts of lower orders
آ آ آ آ Stood a ï؟½terraceï؟½ in the city when the current year began,
آ آ آ آ And a notice indicated there were vacancies for boarders
آ آ آ آ In the middle house, and lodgings for a single gentleman.
آ آ آ آ Now, a singular observer could have seen but few attractions
آ آ آ آ Whether in the house, or ï؟½missusï؟½, or the notice, or the street,
آ آ آ آ But at last there came a lodger whose appearances and actions
آ آ آ آ Puzzled Constable Mï؟½Carty, the policeman on the beat.

آ آ آ آ He (the single gent) was wasted almost to emaciation,
آ آ آ آ And his features were the palest that Mï؟½Carty ever saw,
آ آ آ آ And these indications, pointing to a past of dissipation,
آ آ آ آ Greatly strengthened the suspicions of the agent of the law.
آ آ آ آ He (the lodger, hang the pronoun!) seemed to like the stormy weather,
آ آ آ آ When the elements in battle kept it up a little late;
آ آ آ آ Yet heï؟½d wander in the moonlight when the stars were close together,
آ آ آ آ Taking ghostly consolation in a visionary state.

آ آ آ آ He would walk the streets at midnight, when the storm-king raised his banner,
آ آ آ آ Walk without his old umbrella,, wave his arms above his head:
آ آ آ آ Or heï؟½d fold them tight, and mutter, in a wild, disjointed manner,
آ آ آ آ While the town was wrapped in slumber and he should have been in bed.
آ آ آ آ Said the constable-on-duty: ï؟½Shure, Oi wonther phwat his trade is?ï؟½
آ آ آ آ And the constable would watch him from the shadow of a wall,
آ آ آ آ But he never picked a pocket, and he neï؟½er accosted ladies,
آ آ آ آ And the constable was puzzled what to make of him at all.

آ آ آ آ Now, Mï؟½Carty had arrested more than one notorious dodger,
آ آ آ آ He had heard of men afflicted with the strangest kind of fads,
آ آ آ آ But he couldnï؟½t fix the station or the business of the lodger,
آ آ آ آ Who at times would chum with cadgers, and at other times with cads.
آ آ آ آ And the constable would often stand and wonder how the gory
آ آ آ آ Sheol the stranger got his living, for he loafed the time away
آ آ آ آ And he often sought a hillock when the sun went down in glory,
آ آ آ آ Just as if he was a mourner at the burial of the day.

آ آ آ آ Mac. had noticed that the lodger did a mighty lot of smoking,
آ آ آ آ And could ï؟½stow away a long ï؟½un,ï؟½ never winking, so he could ;
آ آ آ آ And Mï؟½Carty once, at midnight, came upon the lodger poking
آ آ آ آ Round about suspicious alleys where the common houses stood.
آ آ آ آ Yet the constable had seen him in a class above suspicion, 
آ آ آ آ Seen him welcomed with effusion by a dozen ï؟½toney gentsï؟½, 
آ آ آ آ Seen him driving in the buggy of a rising politician
آ آ آ آ Throï؟½ the gateway of the memberï؟½s toney private residence.

آ آ آ آ And the constable, off duty, had observed the lodger slipping
آ آ آ آ Down a lane to where the river opened on the ocean wide,
آ آ آ آ Where heï؟½d stand for hours gazing at the distant anchorï؟½d shipping,
آ آ آ آ But he never took his coat off, so it wasnï؟½t suicide.
آ آ آ آ For the constable had noticed that a man whoï؟½s filled with loathing
آ آ آ آ For his selfish fellow-creatures and the evil things that be,
آ آ آ آ Will, for some mysterious reason, shed a portion of his clothing,
آ آ آ آ Ere he takes his first and final plunge into eternity.

آ آ آ آ And Mï؟½Carty, once at midnight, be it said to his abasement, 
آ آ آ آ Left his beat and climbed a railing of considerable height,
آ آ آ آ Just to watch the lodgerï؟½s shadow on the curtain of his casement
آ آ آ آ While the little room was lighted in the listening hours of night.
آ آ آ آ Now, at first the shadow hinted that the substance sat inditing;
آ آ آ آ Now it indicated toothache, or the headache; and again,
آ آ آ آ ï؟½Twould exaggerate the gestures of a dipsomaniac fighting
آ آ آ آ Those original conceptions of a whisky-sodden brain.

آ آ آ آ Then the constable, retreating, scratched his head and muttered ï؟½Sorra
آ آ آ آ ï؟½Wan of me can undershtand it. But Oiï؟½ll keep me oi on him,
آ آ آ آ ï؟½Divil take him and his tantrums; heï؟½s a lunatic, begorra!
آ آ آ آ ï؟½Or, if he was up to mischief, heï؟½d be sure to douse the glim.ï؟½
آ آ آ آ But Mï؟½Carty wasnï؟½t easy, for he had a vague suspicion
آ آ آ آ That a ï؟½skameï؟½ was being plotted; and he thought the matter down
آ آ آ آ Till his mind was pretty certain that the business was sedition,
آ آ آ آ And the man, in league with others, sought to overthrow the Crown.

آ آ آ آ But, in spite of observation, Mac received no information
آ آ آ آ And was forced to stay inactive, being puzzled for a charge.
آ آ آ آ That the lodger was a madman seemed the only explanation,
آ آ آ آ Thoï؟½ the house would scarcely harbour such a lunatic at large.
آ آ آ آ His appearance failed to warrant apprehension as a vagrant,
آ آ آ آ Thoï؟½ ï؟½twas getting very shabby, as the constable could see;
آ آ آ آ But Mï؟½Carty in the meantime hoped to catch him in a flagrant
آ آ آ آ Breach of peace, or the intention to commit a felony.

آ آ آ آ (For digression there is leisure, and it is the writerï؟½s pleasure
آ آ آ آ Just to pause a while and ponder on a painful legal fact,
آ آ آ آ Being forced to say in sorrow, and a line of doubtful measure,
آ آ آ آ That thereï؟½s nothing so elastic as the cruel Vagrant Act)
آ آ آ آ Now, Mï؟½Carty knew his duty, and was brave as any lion,
آ آ آ آ But he dreaded being ï؟½landedï؟½ in an influential bog, 
آ آ آ آ As the chances were he would be if the man he had his eye on
آ آ آ آ Was a person of importance who was travelling incog.

آ آ آ آ Want of sleep and over-worry seemed to tell upon Mï؟½Carty:
آ آ آ آ He was thirsty more than ever, but his appetite resigned;
آ آ آ آ He was previously reckoned as a jolly chap and hearty,
آ آ آ آ But the mystery was lying like a mountain on his mind.
آ آ آ آ Thoï؟½ he tried his best, he couldnï؟½t get a hold upon the lodger,
آ آ آ آ For the latterï؟½s antecedents werenï؟½t known to the police, 
آ آ آ آ They considered that the ï؟½devilï؟½ was a dark and artful dodger
آ آ آ آ Who was scheming under cover for the downfall of the peace.

آ آ آ آ ï؟½Twas a simple explanation, though Mï؟½Carty didnï؟½t know it,
آ آ آ آ Which with half his penetration he might easily have seen,
آ آ آ آ For the object of his dangerous suspicions was a poet,
آ آ آ آ Who was not so widely famous as he thought he should have been.
آ آ آ آ And the constable grew thinner, till one morning, ï؟½little dhraminï؟½
آ آ آ آ ï؟½Av the sword of revelation that was leapinï؟½ from its sheath,ï؟½
آ آ آ آ He alighted on some verses in the columns of the Frayman,
آ آ آ آ ï؟½Wid the christian name anï؟½ surname av the lodger onderneath!ï؟½

آ آ آ آ Now, Mï؟½Carty and the poet are as brother is to brother,
آ آ آ آ Or, at least, as brothers should be; and they very often meet
آ آ آ آ On the lonely block at midnight, and they wink at one another, 
آ آ آ آ Disappearing down the by-way of a shanty in the street.
آ آ آ آ And the poetï؟½s name youï؟½re asking!, well, the ground is very tender,
آ آ آ آ You must wait until the public put the gilt upon the name,
آ آ آ آ Till a glorious, sorrow-drowning, and, perhaps, a final ï؟½bender,ï؟½
آ آ آ آ Heralds his triumphant entrance to the thunder-halls of Fame.
meaning: About: Henry Lawson (17 June 1867 - 2 September 1922) was an Australian writer and poet. Along with his contemporary Banjo Paterson, Lawson is among the best-known Australian poets and fiction writers of the colonial period.
